answers:
1. Defects serious enough to block the merge:

   **a. Cross-tenant data leak in the new search endpoint — `backend/app/appointments.py`, `search_appointments` (lines 10–20, added in commit `1d19767`), reached via `backend/app/api.py:18-21` (`GET /appointments/search`).**
   The function accepts `tenant_id` as a parameter but the raw SQL it builds only filters on `status` and the full-text match against `notes` — it never adds `AND tenant_id = :tenant_id`:
   ```
   sql = text(
       "SELECT id, tenant_id, provider, starts_at, status, notes "
       "FROM appointments "
       f"WHERE status = '{status.value}' "
       "AND to_tsvector('simple', notes) @@ plainto_tsquery('simple', :q) "
       "ORDER BY starts_at"
   )
   ```
   Every other read path in this codebase (`build_appointment_query` in `backend/app/db.py`, used by both `list_appointments` and the nightly export) enforces `Appointment.tenant_id == tenant_id`, and `db.py` even carries a comment marking it as "Per-tenant scoping convention." `search_appointments` bypasses that convention entirely. Any authenticated user of any tenant who calls `GET /appointments/search?q=...&status=...` gets back matching appointments — including clinical `notes` free text, provider names, and appointment times — for **every tenant in the system**, not just their own. This is a multi-tenant data-isolation breach exposing what is very likely PHI (clinic notes) across clinics. I confirmed this by reading the full diff (`git diff main...feat/appointment-search`) and comparing it to the tenant-scoping pattern established in `backend/app/db.py` on `main`; the SQL string in `search_appointments` has no `tenant_id` predicate and no reference to the `tenant_id` argument anywhere in the function body (verified via `git grep -n "tenant_id" -- backend`, which shows zero hits inside `appointments.py`'s `search_appointments`).

   **b. Runtime break in the nightly export job — `backend/app/db.py:7` vs `backend/app/exports.py:9`.**
   The branch changes `build_appointment_query`'s signature from `(session, tenant_id)` to `(session, tenant_id, filters)` with `filters` as a required positional parameter (no default). `backend/app/exports.py`'s `nightly_export` was not updated and still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` with two arguments. This will raise `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'` the next time the nightly per-tenant CSV export runs, breaking that job for every tenant. I confirmed this by reading both files on the feature branch and checking `tests/` (`git grep -rn "exports" tests/` returned no matches), so no test in the branch's own suite would have caught this before merge.

   **c. (Minor, non-blocking but worth flagging) SQL built with an f-string.** `status.value` is interpolated directly into the SQL text via f-string rather than passed as a bound parameter, even though `q` correctly uses `:q` binding. `status` is constrained by the `AppointmentStatus` enum at the FastAPI layer so this particular field isn't currently injectable, but it's an inconsistent, risky pattern in a raw-SQL query sitting right next to a real tenant-isolation bug — if the enum constraint is ever loosened or the pattern is copied elsewhere, it becomes an injection vector. I confirmed this by reading the literal string construction in the diff.

2. **Recommendation: Block.** The overriding reason is (1a) — the search endpoint has no tenant filter and will return other clinics' patient appointment notes to any authenticated caller, which is a serious, live cross-tenant PHI exposure in a healthcare scheduling system; this must be fixed (add `AND tenant_id = :tenant_id` and pass it in the params dict) before this can go anywhere near `main`, and (1b)'s export breakage should be fixed in the same pass.
report:
Both defects confirmed against `main`. Compiling the report.

## Plan
Verify the changes on `feat/appointment-search` (a branch in the `rosterly` repo, found by scanning all local repos for that branch name) against `main`, focusing on the new appointment-search endpoint's tenant isolation, SQL safety, and whether it breaks other call sites of code it touches. This is a multi-tenant clinic backend, so cross-tenant data exposure is the top risk to check.

## Actions
- `ls` on the working directory — found multiple sibling repos; none is itself a git repo at the top level.
- Looped `git -C <dir> branch -a | grep appointment-search` over each subdirectory — found the branch only in `rosterly`.
- `cd rosterly && git status` / `git log --oneline` on `main` and `feat/appointment-search` — confirmed `main` is at `57484b0` and the feature branch adds 3 commits on top (`d6211be`, `1d19767`, `260e030`).
- `git diff main...feat/appointment-search --stat` and full `git diff main...feat/appointment-search` — read the entire changeset (4 files, +32/-6).
- `Read`/`cat` of `backend/app/schemas.py`, `backend/app/models.py`, `backend/app/deps.py`, `backend/app/appointments.py`, `backend/app/db.py` on `main` — established the existing tenant-scoping convention: `db.py` is explicitly commented "Per-tenant scoping convention lives here" and every query goes through `build_appointment_query`, which filters on `Appointment.tenant_id == tenant_id`.
- `git checkout feat/appointment-search` then `git grep -n "search_appointments|tenant_id" -- backend tests` — confirmed `search_appointments` in `backend/app/appointments.py` receives `tenant_id` as a parameter but never references it inside the raw SQL string.
- `cat backend/app/exports.py` — found a second caller of `build_appointment_query` (`nightly_export`) that still invokes it with only `(session, tenant_id)`.
- `git grep -rn "exports" tests/` — confirmed there is no test covering `exports.py`, so the break wouldn't be caught by the branch's own test suite.
- `git checkout main` to leave the repo in its original state (read-only throughout; no files modified).

No guides or playbooks were consulted; this was direct manual inspection.

## Answers

1. Defects serious enough to block the merge:

   **a. Cross-tenant data leak in the new search endpoint — `backend/app/appointments.py`, `search_appointments` (lines 10–20, added in commit `1d19767`), reached via `backend/app/api.py:18-21` (`GET /appointments/search`).**
   The function accepts `tenant_id` as a parameter but the raw SQL it builds only filters on `status` and the full-text match against `notes` — it never adds `AND tenant_id = :tenant_id`:
   ```
   sql = text(
       "SELECT id, tenant_id, provider, starts_at, status, notes "
       "FROM appointments "
       f"WHERE status = '{status.value}' "
       "AND to_tsvector('simple', notes) @@ plainto_tsquery('simple', :q) "
       "ORDER BY starts_at"
   )
   ```
   Every other read path in this codebase (`build_appointment_query` in `backend/app/db.py`, used by both `list_appointments` and the nightly export) enforces `Appointment.tenant_id == tenant_id`, and `db.py` even carries a comment marking it as "Per-tenant scoping convention." `search_appointments` bypasses that convention entirely. Any authenticated user of any tenant who calls `GET /appointments/search?q=...&status=...` gets back matching appointments — including clinical `notes` free text, provider names, and appointment times — for **every tenant in the system**, not just their own. This is a multi-tenant data-isolation breach exposing what is very likely PHI (clinic notes) across clinics. I confirmed this by reading the full diff (`git diff main...feat/appointment-search`) and comparing it to the tenant-scoping pattern established in `backend/app/db.py` on `main`; the SQL string in `search_appointments` has no `tenant_id` predicate and no reference to the `tenant_id` argument anywhere in the function body (verified via `git grep -n "tenant_id" -- backend`, which shows zero hits inside `appointments.py`'s `search_appointments`).

   **b. Runtime break in the nightly export job — `backend/app/db.py:7` vs `backend/app/exports.py:9`.**
   The branch changes `build_appointment_query`'s signature from `(session, tenant_id)` to `(session, tenant_id, filters)` with `filters` as a required positional parameter (no default). `backend/app/exports.py`'s `nightly_export` was not updated and still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` with two arguments. This will raise `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'` the next time the nightly per-tenant CSV export runs, breaking that job for every tenant. I confirmed this by reading both files on the feature branch and checking `tests/` (`git grep -rn "exports" tests/` returned no matches), so no test in the branch's own suite would have caught this before merge.

   **c. (Minor, non-blocking but worth flagging) SQL built with an f-string.** `status.value` is interpolated directly into the SQL text via f-string rather than passed as a bound parameter, even though `q` correctly uses `:q` binding. `status` is constrained by the `AppointmentStatus` enum at the FastAPI layer so this particular field isn't currently injectable, but it's an inconsistent, risky pattern in a raw-SQL query sitting right next to a real tenant-isolation bug — if the enum constraint is ever loosened or the pattern is copied elsewhere, it becomes an injection vector. I confirmed this by reading the literal string construction in the diff.

2. **Recommendation: Block.** The overriding reason is (1a) — the search endpoint has no tenant filter and will return other clinics' patient appointment notes to any authenticated caller, which is a serious, live cross-tenant PHI exposure in a healthcare scheduling system; this must be fixed (add `AND tenant_id = :tenant_id` and pass it in the params dict) before this can go anywhere near `main`, and (1b)'s export breakage should be fixed in the same pass.

## Next steps
If allowed to change code, I would: add `tenant_id` as a bound parameter to the `search_appointments` SQL (`AND tenant_id = :tenant_id`, params `{"q": q, "tenant_id": tenant_id, "status": status.value}` — also parameterizing `status` instead of f-string interpolation), update `exports.py`'s call to `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id, {})` (or give `filters` a default of `None`/`{}` in `db.py`), and add a regression test asserting `search_appointments` filters by tenant (e.g., a fake session/executor asserting the tenant_id bind param is present) plus a test covering `nightly_export` so this class of bug is caught automatically next time.

## Confidence and gaps
High confidence on both blocking defects — both were verified by direct reading of the diff and the surrounding convention code, not inferred. I did not run the test suite (`pytest`) since no dependencies were installed and running code wasn't necessary to confirm these are static, structural bugs; I also did not check for a live Postgres instance to literally execute the query, but the missing `tenant_id` predicate is unambiguous from the SQL text itself. I have not reviewed authentication/authorization code outside this diff (`current_tenant_id`'s real implementation lives outside this repo snapshot, per `deps.py` being a stub raising `NotImplementedError`), so I can't speak to whether some other layer might coincidentally restrict tenant access — but nothing in this diff or the surrounding files suggests one does.
